text: The surgeon incises the peritoneum and proceeds posterior to the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), carefully peeling the mesentery and the descending sigmoid colon off the retroperitoneum. The ureter is pushed downward while the colon is elevated.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum and dissects posterior to the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), peeling the mesentery of the descending and sigmoid colon off the retroperitoneum. The ureter is pushed down while vessels are moved downward and the colon is elevated.

The surgeon elevates Toltz fascia, separating anatomical structures with the kidney positioned inferiorly, creating a clear anatomical relationship before dividing the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) or inferior mesenteric vein (IMV).

The surgeon elevates the Toltz fascia, separating anatomical structures with the kidney moving downward, creating a clear anatomical relationship before dividing the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) or inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). The visual arrangement is emphasized as a key reference point.
The tip-up grasper elevates the recto-sigma junction, preparing for the infra-IMA approach. The surgeon focuses on identifying the precise location for the incision.
The tip-up grasper is used to elevate the recto-sigmoid junction, preparing the area for incision. The surgeon focuses on identifying the precise location for the incision.
The surgeon jiggles the peritoneum, attempting to predict the junction between the mesorectum and the retroperitoneum. The peritoneum is visible as a thin covering over these anatomical structures.
The surgeon manipulates the peritoneum to identify the junction between the mesorectum and the retroperitoneum, highlighting the thin covering of the peritoneum over these structures.
The surgeon visualizes the junction between the mesorectum and the retroperitoneum through the opaque peritoneum, preparing to make an incision at this junction to enter the avascular plane.
The surgeon visualizes the junction between the mesorectum and the retroperitoneum through the opaque peritoneum, preparing to make an incision at this junction to enter the avascular plane.
The surgeon jiggles the peritoneum to enter the avascular plane, revealing nerves as small, bloody-looking tendrils descending. A small bridging vessel is also visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon jiggles the peritoneum to enter the avascular plane, revealing small bloody tendrils representing nerves that are pushed down to preserve them. A bridging vessel is also pushed down, and the surgeon moves leftward to identify the left ureter.
The surgeon carefully pushes down the bridging nerve to preserve it, while navigating towards the left side to identify the left ureter, which comes into view.

The surgeon initiates a medial to lateral dissection, revealing consistent anatomical relationships regardless of patient BMI. The anatomical structures come into view, demonstrating reproducible landmarks at this level.

The video shows the duodenal-jejunal junction with the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) visible just below the duodenum. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is seen parallel to the IMV, entering the fatty covering of the para-aortic tissue. The left kidney is visible, and the pancreas is located just above the transverse colon.

The video shows the duodenal-jejunal junction with the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) positioned just below the duodenum. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is visible, entering the fatty covering of the para-aortic tissue. The left kidney is discernible, and above the transverse colon, the pancreas is also visible.

The surgeon attempts to position the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) at a right angle to the aorta, but the patient's anatomy causes a deviation. The left ureter is visible, squiggling in the retroperitoneum, surrounded by areolar tissue, which is the target for incision.

The surgeon attempts to position the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) at a right angle to the aorta, but notes the angle is different due to the patient's pelvic anatomy. The left ureter is visible, squiggling in the retroperitoneum, surrounded by areolar tissue, which is the target for incision.

The surgeon uses the tip-up grasper with the third arm to hold tissue in a northward direction while encircling and circumferentially dissecting the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) immediately distal to its takeoff from the aorta. The Firefly imaging mode is activated to enhance visualization.

The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper with the third arm to hold the tissue in a northward direction, encircling and circumferentially dissecting the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) immediately distal to its takeoff.

The surgeon activates the Firefly imaging system to visualize the indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence, aiding in the identification of the left ureter, which is encased by an obstructing tumor. The ICG highlights the stent placed for ureter identification during the procedure.

The surgeon carefully removes tension from the vessel, leaving a cuff of at least one to two centimeters. The peritoneum and lymph nodes are being stripped off the vessel, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.

The surgeon carefully removes tension from the vessel, leaving a cuff of at least one to two centimeters. The peritoneum and lymph nodes are stripped off the vessel, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.

The surgeon uses the robotic system to lift the tissue anteriorly, brushing it down while adjusting the camera view for better visualization. Despite the patient's slender build, the anatomical structures remain consistent.

The surgeon observes the thinness of the descending mesocolon, noting its consistent appearance across patients. The next step involves taking down the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) in this specific case.
The surgeon identifies the duodenal-jejunal junction and the pancreas, then proceeds to divide the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) below the duodenum, ensuring a one-centimeter cuff is left intact.

The surgeon identifies the duodenal-jejunal junction and the pancreas, while dividing the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) below the duodenum, leaving at least a one-centimeter cuff. The next step involves taking down the splenic flexure.
The surgeon manipulates the peritoneum, identifying the junction between the yellowish fat of the mesorectum and the palish, salmon-colored retroperitoneal fat, using a medial to lateral approach.
The surgeon manipulates the peritoneum, moving from a medial to lateral approach, while identifying anatomical landmarks in a more realistic patient.

The surgeon identifies the junction between the yellowish fat of the mesorectum and the palish, salmony, pink retroperitoneal fat, observing the tension and development of a clear section as the tissues are manipulated.

The surgeon identifies the junction between the mesorectum and retroperitoneum, observing the tension and development of a clear section. An incision is made, exposing the areolar tissue and entering the pre-sacral space.

The surgeon makes an incision, exposing the areolar tissue and entering the presacral space. The dissection proceeds from medial to lateral below the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), peeling the IMA off the aorta while searching for the left ureter.

The surgeon begins the medial to lateral dissection below the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), entering the pre-sacral space. The dissection progresses proximally, peeling the IMA off the aorta while searching for the left ureter. Areolar tissue is visibly dissected during this process.

The surgeon passively pushes and lifts the areolar tissue, using the left hand to hook and pull it back towards the eleven o'clock position on the screen. The ureter, hypogastric nerves, and other critical structures are being carefully dissected and pushed down, while the retroperitoneal fat is also being moved downwards. The aorta is visible in the surgical field as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon uses their left hand to passively push and lift the retroperitoneal fat, hooking and pulling it back towards the eleven o'clock position on the screen. The ureter and hypogastric nerves are carefully tended to, while critical structures are pushed down, revealing the aorta.

The surgeon speeds up the video to demonstrate the sub-IMV approach, tenting up the inferior mesenteric vein by lifting the mesenteric fat towards the twelve o'clock orientation. The IMV is clearly visible, and the surgeon incises immediately posterior to it, pushing down the retroperitoneal attachments to access the avascular plane.

The surgeon speeds up the video to demonstrate the sub-IMV approach, tenting up the inferior mesenteric vein by lifting the mesenteric fat into a twelve o'clock orientation. The IMV is clearly visible, and the surgeon incises immediately posterior to it, pushing down the retroperitoneal attachments to access the avascular plane.

In the retroperitoneal space, the surgeon elevates the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) while pushing down critical structures. The sub-IMV approach is used to connect the sub-IMV incision to the supra-IMA incision, advancing into the supra-IMA space.

In the retroperitoneal space, the surgeon elevates the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) while pushing down critical structures to access the supra-IMA space. The sub-IMV approach is used to connect the incision points between the sub-IMV and supra-IMA areas.

The surgeon skeletonizes the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), revealing a T-shaped configuration with the aorta positioned below. The left colic artery is visible heading upwards, while the superior hormonal artery is seen heading downwards.

The surgeon fast-forwards to show the skeletonization of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), creating a T-shaped configuration with the aorta visible below. The left colic artery is seen heading upwards, while the superior hormonal artery is directed downwards. The IMA is positioned centrally, and the surgeon continues to work on skeletonizing it.

The surgeon continues the dissection by skeletonizing the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), creating a T-shaped configuration where the IMA branches off the aorta. The base of the T represents the IMA's insertion onto the aorta, while the upper arms show bifurcation points.

The surgeon employs a sub-IMA approach combined with supra-IMA and sub-IMV dissections to skeletonize the area, creating a T-shaped configuration. The base of the T represents the insertion of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) onto the aorta, while the upper arms depict the bifurcations of the IMA.

The surgeon performs a bailout maneuver by moving laterally when the ureter cannot be found from a sub-IMA approach. The recto-sigma junction is tented anteriorly, revealing the bare area above the IMA pedicle.

The surgeon tents the recto-sigma junction anteriorly to expose the bare area above the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). Using the left hand, the surgeon grasps the bare area and incises it, revealing the retroperitoneal space and the left ureter. Gonadal vessels are also visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses their left hand to grasp and manipulate tissue, revealing the left ureter within the retroperitoneal space. The ureter is partially obscured by gonadal vessels and surrounding vasculature, which are carefully avoided during exposure.
The surgeon identifies the left ureter, partially obscured by gonadal vessels and retroperitoneal vasculature, and begins to expose it further for better visualization.

The surgeon demonstrates the supra-IMA approach, highlighting the anatomical configuration and spatial orientation of the IMA relative to the aorta, with visible bifurcation of the left colic artery and superior hormonal artery.

The surgeon demonstrates the supra-IMA approach, highlighting the anatomical area above the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) as part of a bailout maneuver when the ureter is not visible from a sub-IMA approach.

The video shows the duodenum on the left side of the screen, with the aorta visible and the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) branching off from it. The surgeon tents the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) anteriorly in a cachectic patient undergoing a total colectomy for ulcerative colitis.

The video shows the duodenum positioned to the left of the screen, with the aorta visible and the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) branching off from it. The surgeon tents the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) anteriorly in a cachectic patient undergoing a total colectomy for ulcerative colitis.

The surgeon uses cautery to make an incision immediately posterior to the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), incising the areolar tissue. The mesentery is swept anteriorly, revealing the underlying structures.

Using cautery, the surgeon makes an incision immediately posterior to the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), incising the areolar tissue and sweeping the mesentery anteriorly. The thin tissue planes are visible, highlighting the delicate nature of the descending mesocolon and surrounding structures.

The surgeon enters a vascular plane located posteriorly, revealing thin tissue planes. The descending mesoclonal appears thin, and caution is advised to avoid inadvertently entering the mesentery or kidney due to the delicate nature of the mesenteric tissue.
The surgeon elevates the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) off the duodenum, entering an avascular plane. The pancreas is identified as the dissection progresses in this thin patient.

The surgeon elevates the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) off the duodenum, entering an avascular plane. The pancreas is identified in the surgical field, visible despite the patient's thin body habitus.

The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) at its insertion point on the lower border of the pancreas. A window is created around the IMV, and it is skeletonized in preparation for division.
The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) as it inserts at the lower border of the pancreas. A window is created around the IMV, and it is skeletonized before being divided.
